entries.title: The coach stopped querying its own database by hand
entries.summary: A new get_training_plan_progress tool returns the full graded training plan in one call, so the agent answers plan questions with a clean table instead of dropping to raw SQL. Shipped with a prompt-and-instructions nudge to prefer the structured tools, after a full design and review pass.

entries.title: The brief got 3x faster by measuring, not guessing (v0.6.0)
entries.summary: The daily fitness brief dropped from ~6 minutes to ~80 seconds with equal-or-better quality, after measurement killed the fancy parallel plan and pointed at a one-line reasoning-effort lever.

entries.title: Local Fitness goes agent-first — and a live siege earns its keep — v0.5.0
entries.summary: Re-architected local-fitness from a server-runs-Claude app into an agent-first one — the web server now holds zero inference and the coach lives in an MCP client — then ran a live quality-gate + siege against the running container that caught (and I fixed) a real Fatal in the SQL tool. Shipped v0.5.0.

entries.title: The fitness MCP becomes the primary interface (v0.3.0)
entries.summary: Exposed the fitness data and coaching over MCP so I can query it straight from Claude, instead of the synthesis living only inside the server's brief loop. Adds a coach prompt, schema and brief resources, a one-call daily_snapshot tool, miles formatting, and a write surface for observations and manual workouts.

entries.title: Shipped training plans end-to-end — then let a measurement gate kill a latency optimization
entries.summary: Built the full goal-driven training-plans feature (own tab + daily-brief integration + draft-only AI authoring) and cut v0.2.0, then ran a latency pass on the agent loop — keeping the safe wins and reverting the brief pre-fetch when its live gate proved it didn't help.

entries.title: Treating my fitness agent like real code: eval, tests, CI, and v0.1.0
entries.summary: Gave local-fitness a real quality pipeline — a prompt scorer, a tested core behind a coverage gate, CI, and an auto-release that shipped v0.1.0 — then rewrote the README so it actually reads like an open-source project.
